titleStmt.title: The Ambassador in the United Kingdom ( (Winant) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Ambassador in the United Kingdom ( Winant ) to the Secretary of State
div.p:
7473. From Mann for Pehle WRB. This evening BrowneSidney H. Browne, Second Secretary of Embassy and Consul. and I at request of Malog [Mason], Chief of Refugee Department of Foreign Office, attended a meeting at his office at which Sir Herbert Emerson was also present. Mason read a telegram received SaturdaySeptember 9. from British Minister in Bern a paraphrase of which is as follows:
“The Swiss Government understand from the German Government that the latter are not ready to allow a party of 2100 Jews from Hungary to leave the country if they are to go to Palestine since they do not wish to disturb the Arabs. They would, however, if the group were to have a destination in British or American territory, view their departure more favorably.
Moreover, the German Ministry for Foreign Affairs have requested that the Swiss Legation at Budapest should hand a nominal list to the German Legation there stating that on security grounds, they cannot allow the departure of the group until each individual case has been examined.
The Swiss Government wish to know whether instructions to this effect should be sent to their Legation at Budapest.”
There was no indication that McClelland was advised of the foregoing.

This German move cuts at the very heart of the joint declaration of the American and British Governments since it rejects the offer to place Jews coming from Hungary in neutral and United Nations territory and substitutes instead American or British territory excluding Palestine. While composition of 2100 is not known, it is presumed that they are principally holders of Palestine certificates and acceptance of the offer as made would put American and British
Governments in position of invalidating Palestine certificates at least temporarily even if condition were accepted only for duration of war. Failure to accept German conditions with concrete offer puts Germans in position to say they would let Jews go but we would not take them. Also possible that if conditions accepted in this case, they will be made applicable to all others seeking to escape. Presumably, the purpose of the German maneuver is to embarrass the two Governments.

British propose to instruct their Minister in Bern to reply to Swiss Government along following lines:
“It is assumed that security check is for purpose of expediting and not delaying departure of Jews. With that understanding which Swiss should emphasize, there is no objection to putting list in hands of Germans provided Swiss first assure themselves that Germans will not use such list for purposes of victimizing prospective evacuees. It is assumed that list will show that persons named thereon are holders of Palestine certificates. British Government has no intention of taking any action which would invalidate such certificates. Please ascertain from Swiss if they are prepared to give temporary haven to group with the maintenance guarantees previously given until such time as they can be evacuated. If so, they should advise the Germans that they have agreed to give temporary haven to the 2100 refugees.”

Obviously the proposed reply does not meet the German conditions. It might stand some small chance of success. However, it is altogether possible that the Germans would ask the final destination of the Jews or that the British position with regard to Palestine certificates would be taken for granted in the absence of a statement to the contrary. The British understandably feel that they must preserve the validity of Palestine certificates in view of public and Jewish opinion.

Browne and I attempted to ascertain what were the possibilities of removing the 2100 from Switzerland to British or American soil. We could get no suggestions from Mason who said he would have to consult the Colonial Office to ascertain if any British soil were available. Consequently, it is safe to say that if the German conditions were accepted even temporarily, aside from the other complications, British or American soil means only the latter. Sir Herbert continually asked if we were prepared “to open more camps like the one at Oswego”.

Until I know how far our Government is prepared to go in this matter, it is difficult to make recommendations. I believe the best solution would be an offer on our part of the nature of that resulting in Fort Ontario camp, making clear that such refuge was only for the duration of the war and that after the war, such refugees might return to their own land or a land of their choice willing to accept
them. It seems doubtful that the 2100 could be evacuated before the end of the war but it is possible. Also their evacuation through France might raise problems with the military.

Urgent reply requested giving Board’s and Department’s views on suggested reply or any alternative proposals which our Government cares to put before British. They will not reply until your views are received. Should suggested reply be acceptable, it should not be transmitted until the two Governments are in agreement concerning answer should Germans insist on knowing destination of group. Emerson agrees with proposed British reply and their stand on Palestine certificates. He considers it most important that the two Governments agree on all action to be taken before any reply is made.
Please instruct McClelland to repeat to me important messages to Board and it would be appreciated if Board would repeat replies. [Mann.]
